did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) to generate synthetic skin cancer images for data augmentation and a fine-tuned ResNet18 deep learning network for skin cancer classification on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) for synthetic image generation and evaluating its performance on the ISIC 2018 dataset using a fine-tuned ResNet18 classifier. It includes specific methodology, hyperparameters, results tables, and comparisons with other methods, which are characteristic of a research study rather than a review.

title: Synthetic Images Generation Using Conditional Generative Adversarial Network for Skin Cancer Classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of data imbalance in skin cancer classification by proposing a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) to generate high-resolution synthetic dermoscopic images for data augmentation. The CGAN model was trained on the imbalanced ISIC 2018 dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven skin cancer types, to produce realistic synthetic samples for minority classes. These augmented images were then used to fine-tune a ResNet18 deep learning network for multi-class skin cancer classification. The evaluation compared the classification performance of the model trained on augmented data against the original imbalanced dataset and other state-of-the-art methods. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed CGAN-augmented ResNet18 achieved superior classification metrics, including an accuracy of 94.1%, compared to the baseline original data performance of 89.4%. The authors conclude that CGAN is an effective and efficient approach for mitigating class imbalance and improving skin cancer detection systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven skin cancer types in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification. Original count: 1113 images; Augmented count: 1113 images.

all_task_types.target: High-resolution synthetic dermoscopic skin cancer images
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) to generate synthetic images for data augmentation. The generator produces realistic images conditioned on class labels to address class imbalance in the ISIC 2018 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Seven skin cancer types (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The primary evaluation task is multi-class skin cancer classification using a fine-tuned ResNet18 network. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using Accuracy (94.1% with CGAN augmentation), Precision, and Recall on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions, hosted by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. The images are in RGB color space with original dimensions ranging widely (e.g., 600x450), which were rescaled to 224x224 for the study.

use.training: Used as the source data for training the Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) to generate synthetic images for data augmentation. The augmented data was then used to fine-tune the ResNet18 classifier.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the entire dataset was used for the augmentation and subsequent training/evaluation process described.
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the dataset was used to evaluate the classification performance of the ResNet18 model trained on augmented data versus original data.
use.other: Used to demonstrate the data imbalance problem and to generate synthetic samples for minority classes (BCC, AK, DF, VASC) to balance the distribution.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall) and compared against other methods without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018.notes: The paper compares the proposed ResNet18 with CGAN augmentation against several baseline methods and other ResNet variants. The ResNet18 with CGAN achieved an accuracy of 94.1%, precision of 91.5%, and recall of 79.9%. The authors note that while the proposed method's recall is lower than [22], its accuracy and precision are higher. They also mention that ResNet50 and ResNet101 with CGAN showed similar performance but took more execution time. The recall of the proposed method could be further improved by adding more samples to minority classes (DF and VASC).

architecture_used.main_model: Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) for image generation; ResNet18 for classification
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet18 was pre-trained on ImageNet (implied by 'achieved high classification rate in ILSVRC' and standard practice for 'fine-tuned' ResNet, though explicit pretraining weights file not named); CGAN was trained from scratch on ISIC 2018.

generator.structure: Three blocks of transposed convolutional layers, Batch Normalization (BN), and ReLU activation. Includes a 'ProjectandShape' layer (fully connected upscaling) to match input vector size to 224x224.
generator.input: Random vector from Gaussian distribution + class label (encoded as numerical index)

discriminator.structure: Three sets of blocks. First block: Convolutional layer + Leaky ReLU. Next two blocks: Convolutional layer + BN + Leaky ReLU. Final layer: Convolutional layer for binary classification.

training_details.optimizer: Adam (implied by 'Gradient decay factor' and 'Squared gradient decay factor' parameters typical of Adam)

classification_or_output_head.output: Class probabilities for MEL, NV, BCC, AK, BK, DF, VASC

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SPGGAN (Self-attention Progressive Growing of Generative Adversarial Network)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing/Rescaling: Original images with dimensions ranging widely (e.g., 600x450) were rescaled to 224x224 using bilinear interpolation to reduce computation time.
Color Space: Images were maintained in RGB color space.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to generate high-resolution synthetic dermoscopic images for minority classes to balance data distribution. The generator incorporates auxiliary class label information to conditionally generate images. Synthetic samples were generated for Basal cell carcinoma (BCC: 514 to 1014), Actinic keratosis (AK: 327 to 1064), Dermatofibroma (DF: 115 to 239), and Vascular lesion (VASC: 142 to 253). No augmentation was applied to majority classes (MEL, NV, BK) as they already had sufficient samples.
Original images with dimensions ranging widely (e.g., 600x450) were rescaled to 224x224 using bilinear interpolation to reduce computation time.

conclusions: The proposed Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) effectively generates high-resolution synthetic dermoscopic images to address data imbalance in the ISIC 2018 dataset.

Incorporating auxiliary class label information into the CGAN generator allows for controlled mode generation, producing realistic synthetic images for minority classes (Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Dermatofibroma, and Vascular lesion).

Data augmentation using CGAN significantly improved the classification performance of a fine-tuned ResNet18 network, achieving an accuracy of 94.1%, precision of 91.5%, and recall of 79.9%, compared to 89.4% accuracy on the original imbalanced dataset.
The CGAN-augmented ResNet18 model outperformed several state-of-the-art methods and baseline approaches in terms of accuracy and precision.
While ResNet50 and ResNet101 with CGAN showed similar performance to ResNet18, ResNet18 was selected as the optimal lightweight network due to lower computational complexity and execution time.

The recall of the proposed method could be further improved by generating additional synthetic samples for the most underrepresented classes, specifically Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular lesion (VASC).
CGAN demonstrated superior loss performance compared to traditional GAN, indicating that the use of auxiliary information helps stabilize training and produce higher-quality synthetic images.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 6705 Melanocytic nevus vs. 115 Dermatofibroma), potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or datasets.

No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated only on the same dataset used for training and augmentation, raising concerns about overfitting or data leakage if the split was not strictly maintained.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the improvements in accuracy and precision over baseline methods.

While the proposed ResNet18 with CGAN achieved high accuracy (94.1%) and precision (91.5%), its recall (79.9%) was lower than some comparative methods (e.g., S.S. Chaturvedi [22] with 85.0% recall), indicating a potential trade-off in sensitivity for minority classes.

The authors acknowledge that recall could be further improved by generating more samples for the most underrepresented classes (Dermatofibroma and Vascular lesion), suggesting the current augmentation may still be insufficient for extreme minority classes.

The CGAN generator was trained for only 30 epochs with a batch size of 128, which may be insufficient for stable convergence in GAN training, although the paper claims stable loss performance compared to standard GAN.

The study focuses on multi-class classification rather than binary melanoma detection, which may dilute the clinical relevance for the most critical skin cancer type (Melanoma) compared to specialized melanoma detection studies.

The synthetic images were generated to match the original image dimensions (224x224) but no qualitative visual assessment or expert dermatologist review of the synthetic image realism is reported in the provided text.
